author: Sofia Martínez
title: CSA Farm Email Strategy: Convert Seasonal Subscribers Into Year-Round Members
description: Stop losing 40% of CSA subscribers after harvest. We show organic farms how to use segmented email to boost retention and increase box frequency.

sections.p: We work with 12+ organic farms doing CSA (Community Supported Agriculture) boxes, and the pattern is brutal: you gain 80 subscribers in spring, hit 120 by summer, then drop to 65 by October. That 45% churn rate isn't inevitable—it's usually because farms treat email like a broadcast channel instead of a retention tool. Your subscribers aren't leaving because they don't like your produce. They're leaving because they forget you exist between harvests, or they don't know winter shares exist. Here's how to fix that.

sections.h2: Segment Email By Subscription Stage, Not Just Preferences

sections.p: Most CSA farms send the same weekly email to everyone. Instead, split your list into: new subscribers (joined last 6 weeks), active seasonal subscribers (summer boxes only), lapsed subscribers (dropped off 2+ months ago), and year-round members. Each group needs a different message. New subscribers need onboarding—tell them exactly when to expect delivery, how to store greens, and recipe ideas. Active seasonal subscribers need transition emails 4 weeks before season ends: "Winter is coming: here's what we're growing November-March and why you'll want it." Lapsed subscribers get a single re-engagement campaign (not three).

We helped a 180-member farm in upstate New York segment their list in September and saw year-round conversions jump from 12% to 31% of summer subscribers within 60 days. The difference was sending the right message to the right moment in the customer lifecycle. They weren't selling harder—they were selling smarter.

list: Email 1 (day 1): Welcome + storage/prep tips for their first box
Email 2 (week 3): Share a recipe that uses 4+ items from the box
Email 3 (week 6): Behind-the-scenes farm story (pest management, soil health, staff intro)
Email 4 (week 8): Introduce add-ons (eggs, cheese, bread, jam) with pricing
Email 5 (12 weeks in, mid-August): "Summer ends in 6 weeks—here's your winter box options"
Email 6 (week 14): Customer testimonial from a year-round member + winter box CTA

sections.p: This sequence costs nothing to set up in Mailchimp or ConvertKit and runs automatically every time someone subscribes. The magic is in the timing: you're not selling at email #1 or #2, you're building trust. By email #4, they've opened your emails 3+ times and feel connected to the farm. Email #5 works because you're not asking them to stay with you forever—just move to winter shares, which feel like a natural transition, not a sales push.

sections.quote: We were losing people every September. Now we're keeping 68% into winter boxes. Email isn't magic—it's just showing up when people forget you exist.

sections.h2: Use Behavioral Email Triggers (Churn Warning Signs)

sections.p: Set up two simple alerts in your email platform: (1) If someone hasn't opened any email in 30 days, send them a single email titled "We miss you—here's what's in stock this week." Include a photo of the current harvest and a direct link to renew. (2) If someone skips 2 consecutive weeks, send a "Hey, we noticed you missed last week—next week's box includes X, Y, Z" email. This is a soft re-engagement that works because it's honest and timely. We see 8-12% of skipped subscribers come back after the second trigger email. That's usually the difference between someone churning and someone staying.

sections.h2: Track Email Revenue, Not Just Open Rates

sections.p: Stop looking at open rate. It's a vanity metric. Instead, measure: "What percentage of subscribers who opened email #5 (winter box CTA) converted to a winter box?" and "What's the average box value of people who received all 6 onboarding emails vs. partial sequence?" We've tracked this with 8 farms and consistently see that subscribers who receive the full sequence spend 22% more per year than those who get sporadic emails. One farm attributed $6,400 in additional winter box revenue directly to email segmentation in a single off-season. That's real.
